items.text: petit bourgeois art students, who a few months ago were David Bowie and Bryan Ferry look-alikes – who've read a little art history and adopted some Dadaist typography and bad manners, and who are now in the business of reproducing a fake street credibility
items.context: Jarman's critical assessment of the punk movement in Britain before the release of his film Jubilee; he was reflecting on what he saw as superficiality and inauthenticity in punk culture.
items.publicReaction: The quote reflected Jarman's outsider perspective on punk; his film Jubilee was later rejected by many within the punk movement as phoney, including Vivienne Westwood, though it received acclaim in art and film circles.
items.source: Derek Jarman's personal diary; referenced in Tony Peake's biography and film criticism sources

items.text: I don't particularly want people to like the film.
items.context: Statement about his film Jubilee (1978), made during interviews promoting the politically provocative film made to coincide with Queen Elizabeth II's Silver Jubilee.
items.publicReaction: The statement reflected Jarman's deliberate confrontational approach; the film proved controversial but garnered critical respect for its artistic daring and political engagement.
items.source: The Guardian interview

items.text: We have now seen all established authority...fail to provide any solution.
items.context: Statement about the political failure of established institutions, made in relation to his film Jubilee's critique of contemporary British society.
items.publicReaction: This reflected the radical political stance Jarman was developing; it resonated with counterculture audiences but alarmed conservative critics.
items.source: The Guardian

items.text: an exhilarating change after seven years in cramped Georgian terrace houses and basements... the warehouse allowed me to slip quietly away from the 'scene' which for five years had been the centre of my life – and had now exhausted itself – and establish my own idiosyncratic mode of living
items.context: Reflection on his move to Upper Ground opposite Blackfriars Bridge in August 1969, describing his escape from the London art scene and establishment of independent living.
items.publicReaction: This internal reflection documented Jarman's evolving practice and independence in his artistic pursuits.
items.source: Dancing Ledge (1984) - Jarman's autobiography/diary collection

items.text: On December 22, 1986, finding I was body positive, I set myself a target: I would disclose my secret and survive Margaret Thatcher.
items.context: Jarman's account of his HIV diagnosis and his decision to publicly disclose his status, an extraordinarily brave decision in 1986 Britain when HIV was heavily stigmatized.
items.publicReaction: His public disclosure was groundbreaking and controversial; he became one of the first prominent British figures to openly acknowledge an HIV-positive status, facing both support and hostility.
items.source: At Your Own Risk: A Saint's Testament (1992)

items.text: The slow-witted approach to the HIV epidemic was the result of a thousand years of Christian malpractice and the childlike approach of the church to sexuality.
items.context: Jarman's critique of institutional and religious responses to the AIDS crisis, connecting it to historical religious repression of sexuality.
items.publicReaction: This statement reinforced Jarman's reputation as a fearless critic of religious and institutional hypocrisy; it was controversial but became a key articulation of queer perspectives on AIDS.
items.source: The Art Story; various essays and interviews

items.text: I'm not cowering in this room, I'm going down fighting
items.context: Statement about his film The Last of England (1987), expressing his commitment to political resistance against Thatcherism, Section 28, and the AIDS crisis despite his illness.
items.publicReaction: The statement embodied the fighting spirit that defined Jarman's later years; it resonated with activists and the LGBTQ+ community as a powerful expression of defiance.
items.source: Film interviews regarding The Last of England

items.text: Understand that sexuality is as wide as the sea. Understand that your morality is not law. Understand that we are you. Understand that if you and I decide to have sex, whether safe, safer, or unsafe, it is our decision and you have no rights in our lovemaking.
items.context: Jarman's manifesto-like statement on sexual autonomy and freedom, written as part of At Your Own Risk during his struggle with AIDS, asserting queer rights and bodily autonomy.
items.publicReaction: This became one of Jarman's most celebrated statements, widely quoted in LGBTQ+ activism and literature; it articulated radical sexual politics and personal freedom.
items.source: At Your Own Risk: A Saint's Testament (1992) - back jacket

items.text: All men are homosexual, some turn straight. It must be very odd to be a straight man because your sexuality is hopelessly defensive.
items.context: Provocative statement challenging heteronormative assumptions about sexuality, asserting a queerness fundamental to all sexuality.
items.publicReaction: The statement was provocative and controversial; it challenged conventional sexual ideology and became a key text in queer theory discussions.
items.source: At Your Own Risk: A Saint's Testament (1992)

items.text: I hope boys will carry on falling in love with boys and girls with girls
items.context: Jarman's hopeful assertion of queer futures and continuity of same-sex love, written while living with AIDS.
items.publicReaction: This became an iconic statement of queer hope and defiance in the face of AIDS; widely quoted in LGBTQ+ communities as an affirmation of queer life.
items.source: At Your Own Risk: A Saint's Testament (1992)

items.text: As I sweat it out in the early hours, a 'guilty victim' of the scourge, I want to bear witness to how happy I am, and will be till the day I die, that I was part of the hated sexual revolution; and that I don't regret a single step or encounter I made in that time.
items.context: Jarman's powerful affirmation of his sexual life and the sexual liberation of the 1960s-70s, written while suffering from AIDS at a time when much discourse blamed sexual liberation for the epidemic.
items.publicReaction: This statement was profoundly moving to many in the LGBTQ+ community; it directly countered moralistic blame and affirmed queer life as valuable despite suffering.
items.source: At Your Own Risk: A Saint's Testament (1992)

items.text: Dear William Shakespeare. I am a 14 year old and I'm queer like you. I'm learning art.
items.context: A letter directed to Shakespeare, written as part of Jarman's Letter to the Minister (1992), addressing queer identity and artistic learning.
items.publicReaction: The letter form allowed Jarman to speak across centuries to a historical queer figure; it was seen as a moving articulation of queer continuity and inheritance.
items.source: Letter to the Minister (1992)

items.text: How to make a film of a gay love affair and have it commissioned. Find a dusty old play and violate it.
items.context: Jarman's ironic reflection on how he approached making Edward II (1991), using Christopher Marlowe's 400-year-old play to create a contemporary queer film.
items.publicReaction: The statement became emblematic of Jarman's innovative approach to queer cinema; it was celebrated as a clever articulation of his strategy for subverting canonical literature.
items.source: Queer Edward II; various interviews about the film

items.text: I want to be part of the final alteration in the laws on homosexuality and I want to tend my garden.
items.context: Jarman's dual articulation of his political and personal priorities, written during his illness when he was simultaneously engaged in activism and gardening at Dungeness.
items.publicReaction: This statement captured the dual nature of Jarman's later years—political engagement and retreat into nature; it resonated with many as an expression of integrated life.
items.source: Modern Nature; various published interviews

items.text: My garden's boundaries are the horizon.
items.context: Poetic reflection on Jarman's famous garden at Prospect Cottage, Dungeness, which he created on barren shingle after his HIV diagnosis, expressing expansive vision.
items.publicReaction: The statement became famous in relation to Jarman's gardening; it expressed his transformative artistic vision and has become iconic in discussions of queer space-making.
items.source: Modern Nature: Journals 1989-1990 (published 1991); later writings

items.text: I want to release some of the anger which everyone with HIV feels.
items.context: Jarman's statement about creating his painting Ataxia (1993), expressing the emotional and political intensity of his artistic response to AIDS.
items.publicReaction: The statement articulated the rage and grief of the AIDS crisis; it was widely understood as giving voice to collective trauma in the queer community.
items.source: Various interviews and artist statements about 1993 paintings

items.text: To make a film about illness is dangerous because the parameters of any epidemic change rapidly... Since the virus is essentially invisible, the Blue reflects it more accurately.
items.context: Jarman's artistic statement about Blue (1993), his final film made while nearly blind from AIDS complications, explaining his radical aesthetic choice of a monochromatic blue screen.
items.publicReaction: The statement was widely cited as articulating a new approach to representing AIDS through cinema; critics saw it as a profound artistic innovation.
items.source: Blue film foreword and production notes (1993)

items.text: Yes, I've been involved with OutRage and we're a nice gang of people... The situation here is very divided between people who feel we should all behave and speak to the government and all that malarkey and other people who are activists. And I'm on the side of the activists... I don't believe in this whole idea of sitting down and having tea with Mr. Major and all. I think it's all ridiculous.
items.context: Jarman's final public interview in February 1994 (Art and Understanding), discussing his involvement with the direct action group OutRage! and his rejection of mainstream accommodationist approaches.
items.publicReaction: The statement became Jarman's final public articulation of his radical activism; it reinforced his reputation as an uncompromising activist engaged in direct action.
items.source: Final interview, Art and Understanding magazine (February 1994)

items.text: Sexuality colors my politics.
items.context: Jarman's concise statement on the relationship between his personal identity and political engagement, articulating how his queerness fundamentally shaped his artistic and activist work.
items.publicReaction: This became a signature statement characterizing Jarman's integrated approach to art and politics; it was widely quoted in discussions of queer aesthetics and activism.
items.source: Various interviews and essays; quoted in The Art Story

items.text: People say to me, 'You make fantastic films' and I say, 'No, I make documentaries.'
items.context: Jarman's ironic statement about his filmmaking practice, challenging conventional distinctions between documentary and fiction by emphasizing the documentary aspects of his work.
items.publicReaction: The statement was embraced by film scholars and Jarman advocates as capturing his innovative approach; it challenged traditional genre boundaries.
items.source: Bergen Filmklub; Wikiquote
